author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
I think you're liable to  have strength problems.  Bike rims by themselves aren't all that strong, the strength is in the spokes.  Your setup with two points of pressure close together at the bottom of the wheel, while it appears sensible in that it "spreads the load" as opposed to a single point of contact, will, by its very geometry, produce a massive load on the rim.


Assuming it's a 26" rim, that's a diameter of 559mm , with 2 load-bearing points spaced around 150mm apart (by eye), that gives an arc subtending around 15°, with a height of around 5mm.  For those sorts of angles, it can be basically considered to be a pair of straight lines.


So you have 2 vertically acting point loads at the end points, if we assume a total weight for bicycle and rider of 100kg, and an even spread of weight from front to rear wheel, that's roughly 250N at each point.  That translates to a 500N reaction force at the contact point, also acting vertically, but upwards.  That puts bending moments of 250 * 0.075 = 18.75N.m acting in opposition on either side of the contact point, which is about the torque you can put on a high grade 8mm bolt before it risks shearing.  There's also the little matter of the compressive force you're applying to the rim - it might only be 500N vertically, but along those segments it's pretty massive - the tangent of the angle from centre point to end point is about 1/15 so the compressive force is about 500 * 15 = 7500N.  In plain english, that's about 3 quarters of a tonne.

author: @AdricM
text: can i suggest forgoing the cog belt. and consider just putting rubber grippy wheel on the motor on the rim.  (it could even rep;lace one of your bearings.    as others said. you may need to buy or make the tire tubless.   a friend once made a spray foam filled tire. punched a few big holes on  the opposite side from the stem,  filled the tube with spray polyurethane high density expanding foam, and then waited for it to squirt out  the holes. then let it sorta plug the leaks, and then they introduced more holes, to give it more places for the solvent to get out. .  and more.  it was a goopy mess but when done it worked ok.  i think he made it leak about 30 times with an icepick,  and left it sitting.. eventually he was able to trim off the goo and chop off the stem and ride.   (not as good as pneumatic but he just wanted to never have thorns again and they had not really invented the solid foam airless tires.

author: @Mr_Blinky
text:
I like the principle but I suspect the friction in the bearings/runners would not allow this to be very efficient. However, if this was done using a mag-lev rail system..... That would be slick, but the power that would need! And how to make the rim magnetic enough to hold the load would be equally as big challenge as the power supply. And the electro-magnets would weigh a ton. Maybe that's why we still have the hub and spoke today. Don't let me put you off though. Few projects succeed but often you pick up things that can prove other projects a success. Never give up.

BTW, have you seen google patent US10322827B2 yet? "High frequency gravitational wave generator". That's some proper Sci-Fi stuff right there.

the hubless wheel i saw was on the tv series biker buildoff , the builder billy lane is the guy that thought up the hubless wheel on a single side swingarm (though there are some that deny this claim) , he used a realy expensive and verry wide bearing made for cement mixers , the guy even though he looks like the average biker dude is pretty educated and calculated the forces put on the wheel and made the technical drawings to make sure it would work and be safe
seen two different sort of drives , one is a roller pressed against the tire sitting in the frame between the engine and wheel wish is nearly invisible and might work on an electric bicycle too
the other is a realy big chainsprocket with a huge center hole mounted to the rim and driven from a jackshaft to bring the ratio down enough to actually be able to ride the bike at speed while keeping the engine from over reving

author: @scotttovey
text:
Modifying the rim and having the valve come out the side won't solve the problem with the valve being in the way.

You will need to either modify your bearing casing, or make the rim taller along the sides in order to gain the necessary clearence.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - I have been pondering that and there have been a few good suggestions

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
One solution would be to have an open ended bearing. That is, one side of the bearing would not have a support. 

Actually, it could have a half circle support. 
That should allow for the clearance on that side of the rim.

To create this you would need to cut one side of your bracket down so that your cut line runs through the center of the bolt holes.

That would give the bolts something to rest against while at the same time, reducing the depth of the bracket on that side.

author: @jamesmcpherson3924
text:
Cheers! As a bike mechanic, I'd like to alert you to a couple modes subtle features that might help with this design. Keep in mind that the the spokes under tension support the wheel dynamically at each connection, at all times. They end up providing a huge amount of structure, which makes sense when you take into account the cumulative tension of 32 or so spokes, each holding up to 200 KgF. It's generally understood that the rim is the weakest link in the system. 


You are right that in most cases, supporting the wheel at the bottom will do a pretty good job, but as you turn, the wheel will want to counter bend at about 90 degrees from the applied force (like a pringle chip or saddle.) With the pressure of the tire pushing inwards on the base of the rim, a moderate turn can be enough to push the wheel permanently into a saddle shape. Your 3 points of contact, if spaced evenly should help avoid this deflection and permanent deformation, but you may find that the supports need to be surprisingly rigid and heavy. 


Also, if you impact something with an unsupported portion of the rim, it will nearly certainly deform the rim badly, leading to it locking up as it enters the bearings. Deciding where and how long the bottom support needs to be should be calculated based on where the wheels normally contact during hard turns, and then extend them significantly forward to support potential impacts. Also, wider tires at lower pressures will help reduce stress on the entire setup.


With spare time, I would take a look at some of the studies of wheels on this site: https://www.wheelfanatyk.com/blog/


Very informative and I'm sure you'd find insights to help you continue your adventure safely and productively. 


If it gets there, It might be super awesome to turn the rim into a motor element. Perhaps with permanent magnets, and even using your "new motor design" idea.

replies.text: nice comments mate  - I actually just plucked the design from an already produced production bike - here it is https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WIVeemZ0_e4 - I am not a designer by any means - I just like cool stuff it is possible to build at home

author: @satibel
text:
if you aren't going with 4 inch tires, you'll probably need some suspension, or you might have a rough ride, since the spokes act as springs.
also, given the closeness of the two points on the bottom, you might want a third (well, fourth) point of contact to avoid slip out if you don't put the other one straight up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @sailingoctopus1
text: Instead of a toothed belt and a motor, couldn't you fix a series of neodymium permanent magnets around the wheel and have a set of electromagnets mounted where your motor was going to be. By synchronising the pulsing of the electromagnets with the passing permanent magnets the wheel becomes the rotor of your electric motor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice idea mate - i'll probably go with the belt but you could always give it a try
